data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1517 ET [Dow Jones]--WTI crude futures snap a four-session losing streak, but that isn't enough to avoid a weekly loss ahead of an expected OPEC production increase. The group of oil-producing countries meet this weekend. Energy analysts are also watching for a potential impact from the U.S. government shutdown, which could harm economic activity if extended for too long. The front-month contract falls 7.4% this week, to $60.88 a barrel. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com)

1422 ET - The Bank of Canada may cut rates more gradually than expected as senior officials appear sensitive to upside risks to inflation, Capital Economics says. The forecasting firm says the minutes from recent BOC deliberations, ahead of a Sept. 17 rate cut, characterize upward inflation risks in a hawkish manner--emphasizing, for instance, that risks had diminished but "had not gone away." The BOC is likely to be "particularly responsive" to any upside inflation risks, CapEcon says. As a result, CapEcon says that means cutting the policy rate more slowly to reduce upside inflation risks. It projects one more rate cut this year, either later this month or December, and twice more in 1H of 2026. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1319 ET - The federal government shutdown is unlikely to be a major event for municipal bond issuers, Thornburg Investment Management says in a note. However, certain areas are more exposed "in terms of federal workers, federal contracts and federal spending on goods and services," the firm says. Such places, including Virginia, Maryland and D.C., could see a temporary hit on tax revenue due to deferred wages. Thornburg adds that sales taxes could also suffer. If the shutdown extends past 30 days, federal funding for food stamps could be threatened "and states would likely have to tap their own funds to cover the gap," the firm says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1226 ET - These are risky times to jump into long-term Treasury bonds. The expected Fed cut this month won't weaken long-term yields, Harbor Capital's Jake Schurmeier says. Because inflation is still above the Fed's 2% target, rate cuts risk boosting consumer prices. Economic activity is forecast to remain relatively strong, further stoking inflation risks, Schurmeier says. All that makes interest rate hikes more likely in the long run, a reason why yields on Treasurys maturing in 10 years or more are falling much slower than in shorter maturities. As of yesterday, the 10-year yield was down 0.488 percentage point this year, compared to 0.703 p.p. for the two-year. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1216 ET - Alaska Air and American Airlines will be among the most challenged carriers to emerge from the third-quarter earnings season, analysts say. Both are expected to be the only airlines to report a decline in earnings from the same period a year earlier, Deutsche Bank analysts say, adding that inclement weather at key hubs in Seattle and Dallas hurt both carriers. TD Cowen analysts expect Alaska and American will revise fourth-quarter estimates down. The analysts are confident about the two carriers' long-term performance, but think they have less near-term upside than some of their rivals. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1202 ET - The airline industry still looks like a tale of two tiers as companies prepare to report their third-quarter financials, TD Cowen analysts say. United Airlines and Delta Air Lines are poised to continue leading the pack, as they are more exposed to improving corporate travel and long-haul international demand, the analysts say. They also have better-performing loyalty programs and durable demand for premium seating. Airlines that rely more on domestic travel demand will be more challenged in the near-term because pricing for main cabin seats and domestic flights remains soft, the analysts say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1056 ET - The credit spreads on euro-denominated corporate bonds could tighten in the coming weeks as new issuance remains low, Societe Generale's Juan Valencia says in a note. New supply of euro corporate bonds is expected to remain muted during the earnings blackout period throughout October, he says. "We may well see spreads improve further, as anecdotal evidence shows that there is still cash to put to work and the recent heavy new issue volumes have failed to satiate demand for credit." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0929 ET - Oil prices try to recover some of the ground lost this week, as markets brace for increased OPEC+ production. The potential for added supply is combined with concerns that a slowing U.S. economy could dent demand. Meanwhile, a fire at Chevron's El Segundo refinery in Los Angeles has been contained, according to local police. Still, the incident helps lift prices. WTI rises 0.4% to $60.75 a barrel. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0914 ET - Brazilian corporations could soon find it harder to get affordable financing, according to Fitch Ratings. The firm says in a report that about 23% of local market debt in its portfolio was due to mature in 2025-2026. Strong liquidity has allowed companies to refinance debt in past months, with Fitch-rated entities issuing more than 39 billion Brazilian reais ($7.3 billion) in 3Q, the firm says. However, recent credit events, including downgrades, creates "the potential for a shift toward quality that could tighten conditions for weaker credits," Fitch says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0909 ET - Bitcoin could rise to a fresh all-time high next week if the U.S. government shutdown persists, Standard Chartered's Geoff Kendrick says in a note. This year the cryptocurrency has traded alongside U.S. government risks, with a positive correlation to the 10-year Treasury term premium, or yield investors demand for holding these bonds, he says. Polymarket shows odds of 60% the shutdown will last between 10-29 days so a near-term conclusion seems unlikely. "I suspect Bitcoin will rise throughout the shutdown." StanChart expects bitcoin to rise to $135,000 in the near term and $200,000 by year-end. Bitcoin falls 0.2% to $120,456 after reaching a seven-week high of $121,065 Thursday, LSEG data show. It hit a record high of $124,480 in mid-August.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0904 ET - Issuance of new euro-denominated corporate bonds fell modestly this week, but supply remains strong, Societe Generale's Juan Valencia says in a note. New issues amounted to 14 billion euros ($16.4 billion), excluding Friday's transactions, lower than last week's total of 26.6 billion euros, he says. "[Primary market] activity remains healthy despite a clear drop in volumes," he said. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0848 ET - Bond markets are lukewarm, as is the dollar, as Jobs Friday turns into a data void with the U.S. government shutdown delaying the September payrolls release. Economists surveyed by WSJ were expecting job creation to increase to 51,000 from 22,000, with unemployment steady at 4.3%. Other labor statistics earlier this week pointed to continued weakness in hiring amid contained layoffs. The September ISM report on business services PMI, put out by a not-for-profit professional supply management group, and due at 10 a.m. ET, is forecast to repeat August's 52 level. The 10-year yield is at 4.085% and the two-year at 3.545%. The WSJ Dollar Index is flat. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

The 10-year yield is at 4.085% and the two-year at 3.545%. The WSJ Dollar Index is flat. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Anduril and Palantir battlefield communication system 'very high risk,' US Army memo says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Army memo highlights security issues in NGC2 platform
  • Anduril says memo reflects outdated program state
  • Palantir stock drops over 7%

By Mike Stone

The much-needed modernization of the U.S. Army's battlefield communications network being undertaken by Anduril, Palantir and others is rife with "fundamental security" problems and vulnerabilities, and should be treated as a "very high risk," according to a recent internal Army memo.

The two Silicon Valley companies, led by allies of U.S. President Donald Trump, have gained access to the Pentagon's lucrative flow of contracts on the promise of quickly providing less expensive and more sophisticated weapons than the Pentagon's longstanding arms providers.

Military drone and software maker Anduril boasted it had a prototype of the NGC2 communications platform working during a battlefield test just eight weeks after winning the contract award. But the September 5 memo provides fodder for critics who argue that Silicon Valley’s move-fast-break-things ethos may not be the best approach for vital military equipment.

The memo from the Army's chief technology officer about the NGC2 platform that connects soldiers, sensors, vehicles and commanders with real-time data paints a bleak security picture of the initial product.

"We cannot control who sees what, we cannot see what users are doing, and we cannot verify that the software itself is secure," the memo says.

Those concerns have been addressed already as part of the "normal process" of development, Anduril said. "The recent report reflects an outdated snapshot, not the current state of the program," the company said in a statement emailed to Reuters.

A Palantir spokesperson said "No vulnerabilities were found in the Palantir platform."

However the Army internal memo written by Gabriele Chiulli, the Army chief technology officer authorizing official on the NGC2 prototype, said "given the current security posture of the platform and the hosted 3rd party applications the likelihood of an adversary gaining persistent undetectable access to the platform requires the system be treated as very high risk."

Palantir stock was down more than 7% in afternoon trading on Friday. Anduril is not publicly traded, but company founder Palmer Luckey has said a public offering is planned.

A September 30 article on Anduril’s website touted the NGC2 system’s performance during a live-fire exercise in Fort Carson, Colorado: "Soldiers fired 26 live missions with M777 howitzers on Fort Carson's live-fire ranges, running AXS side-by-side with legacy crews. The contrast was visible: one team struggling with delays, the other firing digitally in seconds."

Despite the September 5 memo's critique, Leonel Garciga, Army chief information officer and Chiulli's supervisor, said in a statement to Reuters this week that the September 5 report was part of a process that helped in "triaging cybersecurity vulnerabilities" and mitigating them.

But the Army CTO's assessment, seen by Reuters and first reported by Breaking Defense, comes just months after Anduril was awarded a $100 million contract to create a prototype of NGC2 with partners including Palantir, Microsoft and several smaller contractors.

The memo said the system allows any authorized user to access all applications and data regardless of their clearance level or operational need. As a result, "Any user can potentially access and misuse sensitive" classified information, the memo states, with no logging to track their actions.

Other deficiencies highlighted in the memo include the hosting of third-party applications that have not undergone Army security assessments. One application revealed 25 high-severity code vulnerabilities. Three additional applications under review each contain over 200 vulnerabilities requiring assessment, according to the document.

Palantir and Anduril are leading a new wave of defense firms aiming to transform the U.S. military with cutting-edge technologies, including drones, artificial intelligence, and autonomous systems.

Palantir, a data analytics firm known for its work with intelligence agencies, and Anduril, which develops drones and battlefield software, have seen their valuations surge under Trump’s presidency, as Washington increasingly turns to Silicon Valley-style innovation to counter rising threats.

Anduril recently signed a $159 million deal for an initial prototyping period to develop a night vision and mixed reality system as part of the Soldier Borne Mission Command (formerly IVAS Next) program. Palantir signed an $480 million contract for Maven, an artificial intelligence tool that sifts through images and sensor data to provide battlefield analysis.

data.title: House of the Week: A Spaceship-Like Home in Illinois — WSJ
data.description:
By Sarah Paynter

Bob Hollar was looking for lots of space for his 145-pound dog when in 1996 he visited an open house in Mettawa, Ill. As he turned the corner of the driveway and got a glimpse of the white, circular home known as the Spaceship House, his thoughts turned from "I love this" to "What's that?"

"I was just sort of appalled at first. It really wasn't what I was expecting," says Hollar, who is in his 60s and mostly retired from the healthcare industry.

As time went on, the estate grew on him, especially the pond, the fruit trees and the large yard, which was the perfect size for his Leonberger, a large dog breed. "The house was never my cup of tea, but as time went on, we made it that way."

Hollar bought the house for $525,000 in 1996. Since then, he has owned a number of dogs, and in 2013, he married his wife, Lisa Giles. When she started spending more time at the house in the early 2000s, Hollar began upgrading it for their new life together, he says.

Creating space

The biggest project involved removing an indoor pool that spanned the center of the two-level house. The pool was a "big impediment" for everyday life, says Hollar, and removing it allowed them to reclaim living space.

The couple also put in new wood floors and upgraded the kitchen. Outside, they redid the patio, driveway and landscaping, adding a small waterfall and another patio to the property. Hollar did much of the work himself and says he is unsure of how much they spent on renovations.

Can't-miss features

Hollar and Giles like to cross the roughly one-acre pond's bridge to sip wine or tea on the island, which has a firepit. They enjoy listening to the sound of bullfrogs surrounding the lake.

The property has an extensive vegetable garden where the couple grow tomatoes, cauliflower, brussel sprouts, beets and other vegetables.

Reason for selling?

Hollar and Giles, who also own a home on Kiawah Island in South Carolina, are downsizing their property portfolio. They now own a Vizsla dog named Ivy who doesn't fly, and chauffeuring her between two homes — along with maintaining multiple properties — is too much work, Hollar says.

Market snapshot

Mettawa is a suburban area with a number of horse farms about 35 miles north of downtown Chicago. Homes there typically sell for less than $6.25 million, says listing agent Donna Mercier of Coldwell Banker Realty. Demand for homes in the area is high, but few properties tend to come on the market, she says.

Write to Sarah Paynter at Sarah.paynter@wsj.com

data.title: Johnson & Johnson Stock Climbs As Wells Fargo Boosts Rating And Price Target
data.description:
Oct 3 - Johnson & Johnson stock rose about 2% on Friday morning after Wells Fargo upgraded the healthcare giant to Overweight from Equal Weight. The bank raised its price target on J&J to $212 from $170, suggesting roughly 14% upside from Thursday's close.

Wells Fargo analyst Larry Biegelsen pointed to J&J's valuation as a key driver for the upgrade, noting that risks tied to drug pricing and pharmaceutical tariffs appear to be fading. He highlighted that the company's pharmaceutical segment could deliver stronger growth beginning in 2026, with 2027 expected to outpace the prior year. That trajectory, he said, creates a more compelling entry point for long-term investors.

The move comes just days after Pfizer (PFE) announced a deal with the Trump administration to provide its drugs at a discount to U.S. consumers. Biegelsen expects similar agreements from other pharmaceutical players, which could reshape pricing dynamics across the industry.

For Johnson & Johnson, the combination of easing regulatory pressures, a more attractive valuation, and improving pharmaceutical prospects provided enough for Wells Fargo to take a more bullish stance on the stock.

data.title: Nvidia Expands AI Reach With Fujitsu Robotics Deal, Stock Gains
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA, Financials) shares edged higher Friday after the chipmaker unveiled a new partnership with Japan's Fujitsu to build artificial intelligence robots and set up a broad AI infrastructure in Japan.

At an event in Tokyo, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang joined Fujitsu CEO Takahito Tokita to outline the project, calling it part of an AI industrial revolution. Huang said building the right infrastructure will be crucial for Japan and other economies looking to compete in advanced technology.

The plan sets 2030 as the target year for Japan's new AI base. While no financial details were disclosed, robotics is expected to play a central role. One example under discussion is a collaboration with Yaskawa Electric to create AI-powered smart machines.

The partnership builds on existing work between Nvidia and Fujitsu in areas such as digital twins and factory automation, efforts that have already helped Japan address labor shortages tied to its aging population. Tokita said the new initiative would take a human-centric approach designed to keep Japan competitive in global technology.

For Nvidia, the deal underscores its strategy of linking its GPUs to large-scale AI projects worldwide, moving beyond chip supply to helping build the systems that drive AI.

data.title: Intel Hits 52-Week High at $38 as Momentum Builds
data.description:
On Thursday, Intel (INTC, Financials) shares hit a 52-week high of $38.07, ending a rapid rise that has almost doubled the price this year. The rise brings Intel's market value to $177 billion and shows that investors are once again hopeful about the company's attempts to turn things around.

The rise has been spurred by faith in Intel's plan to grow its product line and make its semiconductor foundry goals stronger. Investors have also liked recent business actions, such as a $2 billion private placement with SoftBank and early conversations with AMD and Apple about possible alliances.

Still, valuations are flashing caution. According to InvestingPro data, Intel's RSI suggests the stock is in overbought territory. Analyst targets range widely from $14 to $43, with Bernstein reiterating a Market Perform rating at $21. Some firms, including Deutsche Bank, have raised their price targets, though they remain below the current trading level.

Despite concerns, the stock's strong run underscores growing belief that Intel can reposition itself in a competitive semiconductor landscape. Market watchers will be looking for confirmation in upcoming earnings and any further updates on its foundry strategy.

data.title: Google Commits $4 Billion To Arkansas Data Center Build
data.description:
Google-parent Alphabet is planting deep roots in Arkansas with a $4 billion investment to build a huge new data center in West Memphis, one of the largest corporate commitments the state has ever seen.

The project will stretch across more than 1,000 acres and be powered by Entergy . Google will foot the entire energy bill, a setup Entergy says will pump over $1.1 billion in benefits back into the state and even help lower power rates for regular customers. To support the new facility, Entergy plans to add a 600-megawatt solar farm and a 350 MW battery storage system in Jefferson County.

Google isn't stopping at infrastructure. It's rolling out a $25 million Energy Impact Fund for local residents to support home weatherization, new energy-saving technology, and training programs. The company will also provide Arkansans with free access to AI courses and career certificates.

This is one of the largest investments ever in our state, Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders said, calling it a turning point for Arkansas's energy and tech future.

It's important to note that Google-parent Alphabet's profit engine keeps humming, with its EPS at $2.31 last quarter and $9.39 over the past year. Moreover, its robust EPS has been climbing at an average 24% annually over the past decade.

data.title: Uber Buys Segments.ai To Speed Self-Driving Push
data.description:
Uber is leaning deeper into artificial intelligence with its latest move snapping up Belgian startup Segments.ai to help sharpen its self-driving technology.

The deal, announced on LinkedIn, brings Segments.ai's data labeling platform under the Uber AI Solutions umbrella. No price tag was disclosed, but the purchase makes clear just how important advanced labeling has become for training autonomous systems.

Uber has been scaling up its annotation efforts in recent months, rolling out a platform that handles massive volumes of editing, translation, and labeling tasks. In its post, the company said it's doubling down on LiDAR and multi-sensor data annotation capabilities, praising Segments.ai's experience with LiDAR tools, its deep technical know-how, and its roster of global clients.

For Uber, integrating Segments.ai is all about speeding up the learning curve for self-driving. Every mile of autonomous driving requires painstakingly labeled data, and this acquisition adds expertise to help Uber keep pace in a crowded race that includes Tesla, Waymo, and Cruise.

Also, Uber's valuation looks a lot leaner these days with its PE ratio sits at 16.6, when pared with $5.87 in trailing EPS along with a stock price near $98. For context, that's remarkably below the 10-year median multiple of 43.

data.title: Nvidia's UAE Deal Hits Roadblock Amid U.S. Pressure
data.description:
Nvidia's blockbuster deal to supply billions of dollars' worth of AI chips to the United Arab Emirates has been stuck in limbo for nearly five months a delay that's testing the patience of CEO Jensen Huang and senior U.S. officials, according to the Wall Street Journal.

The roadblock appears to come from Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, who has conditioned approval of the exports on the UAE committing to significant U.S. investments. Without that sign-off, Nvidia can't ship the chips, leaving the deal frozen since it was unveiled during President Trump's visit to Abu Dhabi in May.

The politics don't stop there. Washington is wary of the UAE's close ties to China, with officials concerned that advanced AI hardware could be funneled into Beijing's tech ambitions. That has made the deal a flashpoint between business opportunity and national security.

For Nvidia, already navigating U.S. export restrictions in China, the holdup is a reminder that even as demand for its chips soars worldwide, geopolitics often decides where the silicon can actually go.

It's important to note that Nvidia's bottom line keeps pushing forward with aplomb, with EPS hit $1.08 last quarter and $3.51 over the past year. What's even more impressive is that earnings have grown at an eye-popping clip, averaging 89% annually over the past three years.

data.title: TSLA: Tesla Stock Rises As Cybertruck Debuts In Qatar After Saudi Expansion
data.description:
Oct 3 - Tesla stock rose about 1% at Friday's opening after the company announced it has begun selling its Cybertruck in Qatar.

The expansion comes as Tesla looks to balance slowing demand in the U.S. and China with growth in new markets.

In Saudi Arabia, the company rolled out sales infrastructure including online ordering, pop-up showrooms, Supercharger stations, and service centers. A similar setup is now expected in Qatar to support Cybertruck buyers.

Tesla first entered the Middle East in 2017 through the United Arab Emirates. With Qatar now added, the Cybertruck is officially available in three key markets across the region.

While Tesla does not provide region-specific delivery numbers, filings earlier this year showed about 46,096 Cybertrucks built between late 2023 and early 2024.

The presence of Tesla in Qatar not only strengthens its global footprint but also reflects its strategy to diversify sales avenues as EV competition intensifies worldwide.

data.title: Hitachi Teams Up With OpenAI To Power AI Boom
data.description:
Hitachi (HTHIY) is jumping into the heart of the AI boom with a new partnership with Microsoft -backed OpenAI, aiming to build out the infrastructure that keeps artificial intelligence running.

Under the deal, Hitachi will provide the nuts and bolts for AI data centers everything from power transmission to cooling and distribution equipment while OpenAI brings its powerful language models to the table. Hitachi plans to plug that technology into its Lumada digital platform and explore new ways businesses can use it.

The agreement was made official in Tokyo, where Hitachi President Toshiaki Tokunaga and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman signed a memorandum of understanding. For Hitachi, it's a chance to both expand its role in the fast-growing AI supply chain and bring cutting-edge AI tools into its own ecosystem.

Investors clearly liked what they saw. Hitachi stock surged 10% on Friday in Tokyo, signaling confidence that this tie-up could give the company a fresh edge in both infrastructure and digital services.

data.title: Alibaba's Amap Smashes Record With 360 Million Daily Users
data.description:
Alibaba's Amap just had its biggest day ever. On the kickoff of China's Golden Week holiday, the mapping app hit a record 360 million daily active users, the company announced Thursday. The news helped push Alibaba shares up 4.6% in morning trading.

Golden Week is one of the busiest travel and shopping periods in China, overlapping this year with both National Day and the Mid-Autumn Festival. It also gave Amap the perfect stage to roll out its newest AI tools. Its virtual assistant, Xiaogao, processed more than 2.6 billion user requests on October 1, while a new AI safety system, Eagle Eye Guardian, flagged 290 million unusual driving behaviors. To boost local business, Amap also launched Street Stars, an AI-driven ranking system for merchants.

Alibaba is leaning into restaurants too, waiving annual fees for a year to encourage more eateries to join. The push has already attracted a wave of new sign-ups. With Google Maps blocked in China, Amap and rival Baidu Maps dominate the field and Golden Week showed just how powerful that market can be.

data.title: Inside IFFCO's $2B Debt Shake-Up: Rothschild Takes the Helm
data.description:
Rothschild & Co. has taken center stage in IFFCO Group's high-stakes $2 billion debt restructuring, a move that could reshape one of the UAE's most diversified private conglomerates. According to people familiar with the process, Rothschild replaces Alvarez & Marsal as lead adviser, with its initial mandate focused on shoring up liquidity and managing negotiations with creditors. Beyond that, the Paris-based firm is expected to explore new funding avenues and weigh the potential sale of non-core assets.

On the creditor side, HSBC Holdings Plc seen as one of the most exposed banksjoins several Dubai and Abu Dhabi lenders in the discussions, supported by PwC. While still early in the process, the restructuring has the scale to rival other landmark deals in the region. Saudi Binladin Group restructured an estimated $15 billion, while NMC Health and KBBO Group navigated $7 billion and $2 billion respectively. Whether IFFCO reaches a formal agreement remains uncertain, but the scope signals a restructuring that investors across the Middle East are likely to watch closely.

Founded in 1975 and tracing its roots to the India-based Allana Group, IFFCO has grown into a multinational operating in 50 countries, spanning food, packaging, chemicals, and logistics. The company, along with Rothschild, HSBC, and Alvarez & Marsal, has declined to comment publicly. Still, the combination of liquidity stress, creditor involvement, and the potential for asset sales positions IFFCO at a critical junctureone where execution could determine whether it strengthens its balance sheet or becomes the latest cautionary tale in the region's corporate restructuring cycle.

data.title: Tesla Just Had Its Best Quarter--And Still Lost the EV Crown
data.description:
Tesla just logged a record quarter, but the spotlight is shifting elsewhere. China's BYD (BYDDF) has now outsold the US automaker in fully electric vehicles for the fourth straight quarter, extending its lead by almost 390,000 units in the first nine months of the year. Despite that gap, BYD itself is showing cracks, reporting its first overall sales decline in 18 months in September and a surprise 30% profit drop last quarter after leading a brutal price war at home. The company has since scaled back its annual target to 4.6 million EV and hybrid sales, nearly one million below its original goal.

Tesla, meanwhile, faces its own headwinds. Deliveries hit 497,099 vehicles in the third quarter, but total sales remain down nearly 6% this year and a second consecutive annual decline is possible. The loss of US federal tax credits of up to $7,500 at the end of September could further cool demand in Tesla's largest market. Elon Musk has cautioned that the company could have a rough few quarters without those incentives. While Musk has shifted attention toward driverless technology and the Optimus humanoid robot, he has admitted that these initiatives have yet to move the financial needle.

Analysts now expect BYD to widen its advantage. Bloomberg's survey projects BYD will deliver around 2.17 million battery-electric vehicles this year, compared with about 1.61 million for Tesla. That would mark the first time BYD surpasses Tesla on a calendar-year basis, a symbolic turning point in the global EV race. Yet with both firms battling slower momentum, cutthroat competition, and shifting regulatory landscapes, the next phase of growth could be far harder to win than the last.

data.title: Soybean Futures Fall in Directionless Session — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Soybeans for November delivery fell 0.7% to $10.17 1/4 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade Friday, with traders having difficulty reading the direction of futures amid limited government data.
  • Corn for December delivery fell 0.5% to $4.19 3/4 a bushel.
  • Wheat for December delivery settled unchanged at $5.15 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Lost in the Sauce: Soybean futures paced the decline for CBOT grains, with futures showing little movement as traders are paralyzed between nonexistent data from the current harvest and the will-they-or-won't-they of U.S.-China trade negotiations. "Without China, there is no compelling reason to rally, without farmer selling, there is no compelling reason to break," Charlie Sernatinger of Marex said in a note.

Missing in Action: The government shutdown continues to limit data available to traders. Typical USDA reports are not being published, nor is the CFTC publishing its Commitments of Traders report. In a statement on its website, the CFTC said that it will publish missed reports in "chronological order" after government funding resumes. Analysts doubt the USDA will release its next WASDE report on October 9, RJO Futures' Randy Mittelstaedt said in a note.

INSIGHT

In the Fields: The Wall Street Journal reported Thursday that President Trump is considering an aid package of at least $10 billion for U.S. farmers, especially those affected by China's strategic non-buying of U.S. soybean exports. Right now farmers are more focused on their bumper soybean and corn harvests than on developments out of Washington. "We've been in the fields, we haven't had time to do much of anything," said Robert Geesling, who farms in London, Ohio. Geesling said that while aid for farmers might help his bottom line, it doesn't address problems like high costs for inputs such as fertilizer and seeds.

Opening for Ethanol: California Gov. Gavin Newsom signed legislation this week authorizing the sale of E15-blended gasoline, a motor fuel that contains 15% ethanol. The move is seen as potentially increasing demand for U.S. corn as more ethanol is needed. "If California were to fully adopt E15 across the state, this approval opens the opportunity for up to 600 million gallons of new ethanol demand, which is the equivalent to 200 million bushels of corn," said Steve Kuiper, head of Iowa Corn Growers Association.

Meat Prices Hit Record: Meat prices worldwide rose 0.7% to a new record, and were up 6.6% from a year earlier, according to the latest food price index published by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations. The increase reflects higher bovine and ovine meat prices, while pig and poultry remained broadly stable. The FAO's food price index, which tracks a basket of staple foods, averaged 128.8 points in September, down from a revised level of 129.7 points in August but still up 3.4% from a year earlier. The gauge remains down 19.6% from its peak in March 2022 that was reached after Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

AHEAD

Note: The government shutdown may impact the timing of USDA reports and data releases.

  • The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly Grain Export Inspections report at 11 a.m. ET Monday.
  • The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly Crop Progress report at 4 p.m. ET Monday.
  • The EIA will release its weekly ethanol production and stocks report at 10:30 a.m. ET Wednesday.

Giulia Petroni contributed to this article.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: AI Chatbots Move Toward a Future With Advertising and Online Shopping — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Levine

As the costs for artificial intelligence mount to eye-watering levels, companies are starting to look for ways to pull revenue from free consumer apps like ChatGPT. These AI services may be new, but the monetization playbook goes back to Web 2.0: Advertising and e-commerce

After spending $37 billion building out AI data centers last year, Facebook and Instagram maker Meta Platforms expects to spend around $70 billion in 2025, with more in the coming years. Even with all that capital spending, it still will need more computing power, this week signing a $14 billion cloud contract with CoreWeave that goes through 2031.

Unlike Amazon.com, Microsoft, and Alphabet, which rent out their AI servers in the cloud, Meta's buildout is all for its own use. There are no sales to cloud customers to offset its capital expenditures.

Meta's balance sheet and cash-flow statement are shifting as a result. In the second quarter, Meta had more debt and lease liabilities than cash and short-term investments for the first time as a public company. Free cash flow — operational cash flow minus capex — was down 16% from 2024 in the first half of the year.

Meta is hardly in any real financial risk, but all the AI spending is starting to bite, and investors want to know when they will see a return. CEO Mark Zuckerberg can read the room as well as anyone, and in the first-quarter earnings call in April, he devoted the bulk of his scripted presentation to laying out what Meta stands to gain from all its investment.

He talked about Meta AI, its ChatGPT competitor, and how it will follow Meta's usual path to monetization. "First, we build and scale the product, and then once it is at scale, then we focus on revenue," he said. "But I expect that we're going to be largely focused on scaling and deepening engagement for at least the next year before we'll really be ready to start building out the business here." Zuckerberg returned to this theme in the second-quarter earnings call in July.

For Meta, revenue has always meant ads. Most recently we've seen this path with Meta's X competitor, Threads, which launched in July 2023. By January 2025, Threads had over 300 million monthly users, and Meta began inserting ads.

Now it has that kind of scale for Meta AI, which has at least one billion monthly users. This week Meta announced that it would begin to use their AI chats to better target ads in the company's social media apps. Ads in AI chats themselves may not be far off.

All of this is delicate, given the increasingly personal data users have begun to share with chatbots.

It's possible that ChatGPT maker OpenAI actually beats Meta in crossing the chatbot-advertising Rubicon. OpenAI is still investor funded, and it has committed to future obligations that add up to hundreds of billions of dollars. It needs to show how it intends to eventually become profitable.

While Meta can lean on substantial cash flows from Facebook and Instagram, OpenAI's subscription and user-based revenue are growing quickly but they can't cover ChatGPT's mounting expenses, especially since the vast majority of ChatGPT's 700 million weekly users continue to use a free version of the app.

In May, OpenAI hired Fidji Simo for a newly created role, CEO of Applications, reporting to CEO Sam Altman. Simo rose through the ranks at Meta to eventually run the Facebook app. She then became CEO of Instacart, overseeing the delivery firm's initial public offering. She still chairs Instacart's board and sits on the boards of Shopify and OpenAI.

Simo is an executive with deep understanding of advertising and e-commerce. The company declined to answer questions about her new role or to make Simo available for comment, but she is the exact kind of person a company would hire as it pivots to a free service backed by advertising and e-commerce.

We saw OpenAI's first steps in that monetization journey this past week. In partnership with payment processor Stripe, ChatGPT users can now look for and buy items from Etsy, without ever leaving the chatbot, through a shopping experience ChatGPT calls Instant Checkout.

Millions of Shopify stores will be next to join ChatGPT's e-commerce platform. Once e-commerce shows up, advertising isn't far behind, with sellers looking to get attention.

All of this will feel familiar to anyone that lived through the rise of Web 2.0 sites like Google and Facebook. At first, investors funded the search and social-media sites, allowing the free services to grow by leaps and bounds with users. Once the monetization switch gets turned on, the companies refocused their efforts on advertisers and sellers. Eventually, users and advertisers are all hooked on the platform, and the companies turn their attention to pleasing shareholders.

Expect a similar pattern for AI, with one key difference: The shareholder payoff could take time, with initial profits wiped out by hundreds of billions in spending.

Write to Adam Levine at adam.levine@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Shutdown Effect Likely Small, PNC's Chief Strategist Maintains
data.description:
PNC Asset Management's chief strategist Yung-Yu Ma thinks the latest U.S. government shutdown is unlikely to leave much of a mark on Wall Street, even though it's already in its second day.

In an interview with CNBC, Ma pointed out that markets usually take shutdowns in stride. But he added that this one feels a little different because the labor market looks softer than in past cycles. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq dipped Thursday after touching record highs at the open.

We still don't expect it to have a major impact, but it is a different time when there's a bit more fragility in the labor market, Ma said. He flagged the risk that potential permanent cuts to federal workers could stretch the impact beyond the usual short-term noise.

Even with those worries, Ma kept an optimistic tone. He said robust business spending, steady demand from wealthier households, and long-term momentum in tech and AI are fueling growth. The trajectory overall is favorable, he added, though he expects the Fed to keep labor market weakness on its radar through year-end.

data.title: Police scuffle with protesters outside Chicago ICE facility, arrest several
data.description:
Key points:
  • Federal forces and protesters clash
  • Several people arrested, protesters say they are peaceful
  • ICE actions in Chicago criticized by Democratic officials

By Heather Schlitz

Police arrested several people on Friday as hundreds of demonstrators gathered outside a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility in suburban Chicago that has been the focus of protests since a federal immigration enforcement surge began last month.

Tensions have been high in recent weeks in Chicago as the administration of U.S. President Donald Trump has ramped up his efforts to deport immigrants living in the U.S. illegally, arresting many without criminal records.

This week, Illinois Governor JB Pritzker said the Trump administration had requested the Pentagon to send troops to the Democratic-run state.

Outside the detention facility in Broadview, behind barriers erected by ICE, protesters initially stood peacefully on Friday, singing Christian hymns and Jewish morning songs. Then Gregory Bovino, the U.S. Customs and Border Protection commander who is leading the federal enforcement effort in Chicago, arrived with agents in gas masks and armored vehicles.

Protesters began jeering and scuffling with police, some shouting obscenities.

Illinois State Police officers, some carrying rifles, night vision goggles and clubs, made several arrests, including of an elderly woman, who appeared to hyperventilate as she was shoved to the ground and handcuffed.

"It's outrageous. I'm just out here silently protesting and they're pushing us off the street and sidewalk and they're using violence against us," said Kevin Ryan, a U.S. Marine Corps veteran and Democratic U.S. Senate candidate.

An Illinois State Police spokesperson did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

ESCALATING PROTESTS

People in the Chicago area have staged repeated protests condemning the stepped-up federal presence, and the facility in Broadview, a majority-Black village about 13 miles west of Chicago, has become a focus of them.

On at least four occasions, about a dozen demonstrators sitting on the ground attempting to block ICE vehicles from carrying detainees into the facility have been repelled by heavily armed ICE agents using physical force, chemical munitions and rubber bullets, evoking combat scenes.

Protesters have decried what they call similar heavy-handed policing in other Democratic-run cities, including New York, Los Angeles, Washington, and Portland, Oregon.

Attorney General Pam Bondi ordered Department of Justice officials on Monday to "defend ICE facilities, specifically in Portland and Chicago."

At Broadview, masked ICE agents have regularly fired volleys of rubber bullets, tear gas and pepper powder into the crowd.

Broadview Mayor Katrina Thompson said earlier this week that the clouds of tear gas were affecting residents who lived near the facility, and that a fence ICE had erected without village approval was blocking access for emergency vehicles. The city's police department has opened two criminal investigations into ICE officers' actions at the facility, including allegedly targeting the vehicle of a local TV news reporter.

"We will not allow sanctuary politicians or violent rioters to stop us from enforcing the law," Department of Homeland Security Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin said on Wednesday.

data.title: Apple Removes ICEBlock App After DOJ Raises Safety Fears
data.description:
Apple has pulled the plug on ICEBlock, an app that let users track and report the location of immigration enforcement officers, after facing pressure from the Trump administration.

ICEBlock was pitched as a community resource for people worried about ICE raids, but the Justice Department argued it crossed the line. Attorney General Pam Bondi said the app put federal agents in danger by exposing their movements and potentially making them targets.

Apple defended the move by pointing to safety. We created the App Store to be a safe and trusted place to discover apps, the company told NBC News. Based on information we've received from law enforcement about the safety risks associated with ICEBlock, we have removed it and similar apps from the App Store.

The removal comes as Trump's tough immigration policies keep ICE front and center with frequent raids and detentions. Apple declined to offer additional comment when contacted by Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Boeing Steps Up Hiring Push As St. Louis Strike Drags
data.description:
Boeing is stepping up its backup plan as the machinists' strike at its St. Louis defense plant enters a third month.

In a memo to employees, Dan Gillian, Boeing's top executive in the region, said the company is expanding its recruiting push and warned the standoff could drag on for weeks or months. Boeing is adding more job fairs and advertising to bring in workers, and its first wave of replacement hires focused on munitions production and assembly will begin training Friday.

Union leaders quickly pushed back, calling the move more posturing than reality. They argue the specialized roles take months of training and often require federal security clearances that can take up to six months to secure.

The dispute is also drawing political fire. Senator Josh Hawley blasted Boeing during a Senate hearing this week, criticizing the company for awarding generous executive pay packages while refusing to meet union proposals. With more than 3,200 machinists on strike since August 4, the standoff is testing Boeing's ability to keep its defense operations running smoothly.
